doc_findings:
## FINDINGS

**Lungs and pleura:**  
Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula (previously more pronounced), and basal segments bilaterally.  
In the left lower lobe and right middle lobe, there is regression of previously seen solid and subsolid nodules, as well as areas of peribronchial parenchymal opacity.  
No new nodules are identified; multiple scattered calcifications in the lungs persist, ranging from 2 mm to 6 mm.  
Existing pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:  
- In the right lung:  
  - In the upper lobe:  
    - Nodule measuring up to 4 mm (image 137), subpleural, solid, partially calcified - stable.  
Centrilobular emphysema, greater on the right; in the right upper lobe subpleurally, a bulla measures up to 1.8 × 1.3 cm.  
No pleural effusion.  
The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

**Mediastinum and pulmonary hila:**  
No mass or lymphadenopathy is identified.

**Heart:**  
No cardiomegaly is identified: the right atrium is decreased in size.  
Aortic valve calcification.  
Pericardial fluid with thickness up to 5 mm (stable).

**Great vessels:**  
The thoracic aorta is not dilated; wall calcification.  
The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

**Skeletal structures and chest wall:**  
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes.  
Concrescence of vertebral bodies T1-T2.  
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level up to +117 HU.

**Upper abdomen:**  
In the parenchyma of the right kidney and in its sinus, two hypodense lesions of fluid density with well-defined smooth margins measure 5.6 × 4.0 cm and 4.7 × 3.8 cm, respectively - stable.  
Cholecystectomy - no visible changes in the surgical bed.

## IMPRESSION

1. Compared with January 09, 2026, complete regression of areas of pulmonary parenchymal opacity of consolidation type, as well as solid and subsolid nodules (in the left lower lobe and right middle lobe) - changes were likely inflammatory.  
A partially calcified solid nodule in the right upper lobe (typical of a healed granuloma), and multiple pulmonary calcifications (stable): per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no further CT follow-up is required.  
Pulmonary emphysema with formation of a single bulla on the right (per prior examinations, the patient is enrolled in a lung cancer screening program).

2. Decreased size of the right atrium (visually stable).  
Small pericardial effusion (stable).

3. Aortic valve calcification.

4. Atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with osteophytes.  
Concrescence of vertebral bodies T1-T2.  
Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level.

6. Hypodense fluid-density lesions in the right kidney: per “Management of the Incidental Renal Mass on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee” (2018), CT follow-up is not required.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Moderate centrilobular emphysema. Previously seen bilateral areas of ground-glass and consolidation completely resolved (compared to January 9, 2026). In the right middle lobe, there is complete regression of previously seen solid and subsolid nodules. In the right upper lobe, subpleural solid nodule measures up to 4 mm (image 137), unchanged. No new nodules are identified; multiple scattered calcified nodules and solid micronodules, unchanged. Linear atelectasis in the right middle lobe, lingula, and basal segments bilaterally. No pleural effusion. The trachea and main bronchi are patent.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass is identified. Right lower paratracheal node measures 1.0 cm along the short axis, previously 1.2 cm (on January 9, 2026). Subcarinal node measures 5 mm along the short axis, previously 9 mm (on January 9, 2026).

Heart: No cardiomegaly is identified; the right atrium is decreased in size. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary artery calcification.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated; mild wall calcification. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine with small osteophytes. Block of T1-T2 vertebrae. Decreased bone density in the vertebrae at the T11-L1 level up to +117 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: In the parenchyma of the right kidney and in its sinus, two hypodense lesions of fluid density with well-defined smooth margins measure 5.6 × 4.0 cm and 4.7 × 3.8 cm, stable compared to January 9, 2026 (at CT on November 19, 2024, outside the scanning zone).  Cholecystectomy.
val_impression:
1. Compared with January 9, 2026, complete regression of bilateral areas of ground-glass and consolidation, as well as solid and subsolid nodules in the right middle lobe (inflammatory process). In the right upper lobe, subpleural solid nodule measures up to 4 mm, unchanged. Multiple scattered calcified nodules and solid micronodules, unchanged. No new nodules are identified. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer.

2. Moderate pulmonary emphysema. Pulmonary emphysema on CT is an independent risk factor for lung cancer. Consider enrollment in a low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening program if the patient meets eligibility criteria; today's CT may serve as a baseline for future screening. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has a history of lung cancer, known pulmonary nodules or lung mass, receives routine chest CTs/screening, or is receiving palliative care.

3. Hypodense fluid-density lesions in the right kidney, unchanged. ACR White Paper recommendations (Herts, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2018;15:264-273) suggest the following: Likely benign cyst. No need for further work-up.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
val_protocol:
INDICATION: Other nonspecific abnormal finding of lung field.

COMPARISON: Chest CT on January 9, 2026, November 19, 2024.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 9.79 mGy; total DLP 368.37 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 1.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Pulmonary nodules in series 5 are as follows:

Right upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 5 mm on image 54 (unchanged).
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 96 (unchanged).

Right middle lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 3 mm on image 151 (not previously visualized).

Left upper lobe:
- Solid subpleural nodule measuring 4 mm on image 40 (unchanged).

Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodule along the major interlobar fissure measuring 4 mm on image 104 (unchanged).

In both lungs, solid micronodules measuring up to 2 mm and calcified granulomas up to 3 mm. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No clinically significant pericardial effusion. Moderate pericardial effusion with thickness up to 1.5 cm (greatest in the posterior-basal regions). Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as mild. Calcification of the aortic valve.

Great vessels: Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta to 4.7 cm, mild wall calcification. Main pulmonary artery is dilated to 3.7 cm.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue. Hemangioma of the T8 vertebral body measuring up to 2.6 × 2.4 cm, hemangioma of the T11 vertebral body measuring up to 2.2 × 1.2 cm. Osteosclerotic lesions in the right 3rd, 4th, 6th, and 7th ribs, the left 11th rib, and the T4 vertebra, measuring up to 7 mm (the lesion in the right 6th rib was not previously visualized; the remaining lesions are unchanged).

Upper abdomen: Heterogeneous decrease in liver parenchymal density to 35 HU (more pronounced at the level of the right hepatic lobe, near the inferior vena cava).
doc_impression:
1. Stable micronodules in both lungs and solid nodules in both upper lobes and the left lower lobe; per Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines, no follow-up is required. Solid nodule in the right middle lobe, new compared with July 24, 2024; per Fleischner Society 2017 guidelines, in high-risk patients follow-up chest CT is recommended in 12 months, and in low-risk patients no follow-up is required. Calcified granulomas in both lungs are consistent with prior infection.

2. Moderate pericardial effusion. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.

3. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Demineralization of bone tissue. Vertebral hemangiomas at T8 and T11; no follow-up imaging is required. Stable osteosclerotic lesions in the right 3rd, 4th, and 7th ribs, the left 11th rib, and the T4 vertebra, likely enostoses; no follow-up imaging is required. New osteosclerotic lesion in the right 6th rib.

6. Hepatic steatosis.

val_impression:
1. Stable solid nodules in both lungs. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer. Calcified granulomas in both lungs.

2. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs. Mild calcification of the aortic valve.

3. Dilatation of the ascending thoracic aorta. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic aorta.

4. Dilated main pulmonary artery (3.7 cm), which may suggest pulmonary hypertension.

5. Severe degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Osteopenia. Vertebral hemangiomas at T8 and T11; no follow-up imaging is required. Stable osteosclerotic lesions in the right 3rd, 4th, and 7th ribs, the left 11th rib, and the T4 vertebra, likely enostoses; no follow-up imaging is required. doc_findings:
FINDINGS:

Lungs and pleura: Solid subpleural nodule measuring 1.5 cm in sagittal plane in the supradiaphragmatic portions of the right lower lobe (lung window, image 199/512), unchanged.  
Pulmonary nodules on lung window are as follows, subpleural, triangular and ovoid in shape:  
Right upper lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 32.  
Right middle lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 3 mm on image 57 (not previously seen).  
Right upper lobe:  
- Solid nodule measuring 4 mm on image 71 (not previously seen).  
In the left lung, adjacent to the inferior portion of the left major fissure, several micronodules measuring 2-3 mm persist.  
Mild subpleural scar persists in the posteromedial subpleural region of the right lung apex. A few parenchymal bands persist in the anterior portions of the right middle lobe. A few areas of subsegmental atelectasis in the lower lobes of both lungs. No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. New pericardial effusion in basal portions with thickness up to 6 mm. Coronary calcification is present in the left anterior descending and first diagonal coronary arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. A few calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the walls of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Mild degenerative changes of the shoulder joints and spine with sclerosis of the vertebral endplates and articular surfaces, and mild osteophytes.

Upper abdomen: Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measuring 1.3 cm (previously up to 1.2 cm), density up to +14 HU. Area of hepatic steatosis in segment 4 (not previously seen). Stable calcifications in the upper portion of the right breast.

Thyroid gland: Hypodense nodule in the right thyroid lobe measuring 1.2 cm, and in the left lobe up to 4 mm.
doc_impression:
1. CT appearance of a stable solid nodule in the right lower lobe compared with September 25, 2023. For additional characterization, positron emission tomography/CT may be performed.

2. Several other smaller nodules in both lungs, predominantly in the region of the fissures, including new nodules. Per 2017 Fleischner Society recommendations, no additional CT follow-up is recommended for low risk; for high risk, CT follow-up in 12 months is recommended.

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild.  
The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries.  
Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Mild pericardial effusion, not previously seen.

5. Mild atherosclerosis of the thoracic and abdominal aorta.

6. Thyroid nodules. Per American College of Radiology (White Paper) (Hoang, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2015;12:143-150): no further follow-up is required. Not included in the scan range on the prior study.

7. Left adrenal lesion, likely an adenoma. Stable compared with CT from September 25, 2023. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, no further follow-up is required.

8. Mild hepatic steatosis (not previously seen).

9. Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine and shoulder joints.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In the lung parenchyma, solid nodules are identified, in series 204 presented as follows:
Right upper lobe:  
- Perifissural solid nodule measures 3 mm (image 64), unchanged, requires no follow-up.
Right middle lobe:  
- Solid nodule measures 3 mm on image 114 (not previously seen).  
Right lower lobe:
- Subpleural solid nodule measures 1.4 × 1.2 cm in the supradiaphragmatic portions (image 172). No change in size is observed compared to CT on September 25, 2023, September 9, 2022, however, an increase in density and a change in shape (more flattened) are observed.
Left lung:
- Multiple perifissural nodules up to 4 mm (lymph nodes), unchanged, require no follow-up.
Mild subpleural scar persists in the posteromedial subpleural region of the right lung apex and in the anterior portions of the right middle lobe, unchanged. A few subsegmental atelectasis in the lower lobes of both lungs. No pleural effusion. Mild bronchial wall thickening, a small amount of mucus in the trachea.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. Pericardial effusion with thickness up to 6 mm, previously not seen. Coronary calcifications are present in the left anterior descending and left circumflex arteries, qualitatively assessed as mild.

Great vessels: The thoracic aorta is not dilated. A few calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the walls of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. The main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Bone islands of the right humeral head, 5th and 6th ribs on the left.

Upper abdomen: Lesion of the medial crus of the left adrenal gland measures 1.2 cm (unchanged), density up to +8 HU. Area of hepatic steatosis in segment 4 (previously not seen).

Thyroid gland: Two hypodense nodules in the right thyroid lobe measure 1.5 cm and 7 mm; on the Chest CT on September 9, 2022, they measure 1.3 cm and 4 mm, respectively. Heterogeneous structure of the left lobe.
val_impression:
1. Subpleural right lower lobe pulmonary nodule measures 1.4 × 1.2 cm, stable in size since September 9, 2022, with interval change in density and shape, probably benign. ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up for patients with high or low risk of lung cancer. 
New solid nodule in the right middle lobe measures 3 mm. According to ACR Fleischner Society recommendations (MacMahon, et al. Radiology 2017; 284(1): 228-43) no additional CT follow-up is recommended for low risk; for high risk, CT follow-up in 12 months is optional.

2. Mild bronchial wall thickening, a small amount of mucus in the trachea may represent bronchitis .

3. Incidental coronary artery calcification is present, qualitatively assessed as mild. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Note: Recommendation applies unless the patient has known CAD, prior CABG/stent, trauma indications or intraoperative CTs.

4. Small-volume pericardial effusion, not previously seen.

5. Thyroid nodules with increase in size. Further evaluation with thyroid ultrasound recommended if not already obtained elsewhere.

6. Left adrenal lesion, likely an adenoma. Stable compared with CT from September 25, 2023. Per Management of Incidental Adrenal Masses: A White Paper of the American College of Radiology Incidental Findings Committee, 2017, no further follow-up is required.

7. Focal hepatic steatosis (not previously seen).

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura:
Pulmonary nodules on series 5 are as follows:
Right lower lobe:
- Solid nodule 4 mm, image 150 - unchanged.
Left lower lobe:
- Solid nodule up to 5 mm, image 216 - unchanged.
Left upper lobe:
- Solid nodule 4 mm, image 149 - unchanged.
In both lungs in the upper and lower lobes, multiple micronodules, including calcified, up to 2 mm - unchanged.
In the lungs along the interlobar pleura, polygonal/flattened nodules - on the right measuring 4 mm, image 129, 3 mm, image 128, 6 mm, image 148; on the left 5 mm, image 144, likely intrapulmonary lymph nodes - unchanged.
Mild apical fibrosis on the right and left.
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No mass and no lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. No coronary calcification.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.
A few calcifications of the aortic arch.

Skeletal structures and chest wall:
Combined treatment for right breast cancer, metallic inclusions in the breast and axillary region.
Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

Upper abdomen:
In the liver, lesions are present - hypodense, homogeneous, with density no more than 3 HU in segment 2 measuring 5.1 cm in diameter, in segment 4b measuring 9 mm in diameter - unchanged.
doc_impression:
1. A few solid nodules, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up CT in 12 months.

2. Multiple small micronodules (including calcified) and solid nodules consistent with intrathoracic lymph nodes, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025.

3. Mild apical fibrosis on the right and left; no CT evidence of severe interstitial lung disease, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025.

4. A few liver lesions with benign features, likely cysts, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025; no follow-up is required per Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee, 2017.

5. Moderate degenerative changes of the thoracic spine.

2. Multiple small micronodules (including calcified) and solid nodules consistent with intrathoracic lymph nodes, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025.

3. Mild apical fibrosis on the right and left; no CT evidence of severe interstitial lung disease, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025.

4. A few liver lesions with benign features, likely cysts, unchanged compared with March 11, 2025;  ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: Benign lesion. No need for further follow-up. Moderate hepatic steatosis.

val_protocol:
INDICATION: Menopause, neoplasm of breast, epigastric pain; history of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, interstitial lung disease. Right breast cancer, radiation therapy and lumpectomy in 2016. New onset right posterior chest pain for 2 weeks.

COMPARISON:  Chest CT on 03/11/2026.

TECHNIQUE: Scans were obtained through the thorax without IV contrast, to include contiguous axial images with bone and soft tissue algorithm. Thin slice axial reformat and coronal reformat images were included. Artificial Intelligence assessment was performed separately on the Nodule Prediction workstation. CT scan done according to ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

RADIATION DOSE: CTDI volume 13.30 mGy; total DLP 558.13 mGy-cm.

Prior known CT or cardiac nuclear medicine studies performed in the last 12 months: 0.

doc_findings:
Lungs and pleura: Micro-nodules in both lungs, measuring up to 2 mm in diameter. Fibrotic changes in the apices of both lungs with small calcifications within. Reticular changes in the upper lobes of both lungs. Small perifissural areas of increased density along the interlobar pleura bilaterally. Discoid atelectasis and traction bronchiectasis in the left lingular segments.  
No pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy.

Heart: No cardiomegaly. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification. Mitral valve clipping.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 82 HU, T12 - 57 HU, L1 - 67 HU, L2 - 122 HU.

Upper abdomen: Left-sided Bochdalek hernia. In segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, a heterogeneous round lesion with partially calcified walls and well-defined smooth margins, measuring up to 1.4 cm in diameter, density 16 HU. Common bile duct is dilated up to 9 mm.
doc_impression:
1. Micro-nodules in the right upper lobe. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history. Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

2. Post-tuberculous changes in the apices of both lungs.

3. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

6. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

7. Heterogeneous lesion in segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe (per “Management of Incidental Liver Lesions on CT: A White Paper of the ACR Incidental Findings Committee,” 2017, liver MRI is recommended).

8. Choledochoectasia.
val_findings:
Lungs and pleura: In both lungs, large pleuroapical calcified scarring with lung distortion and pulmonary reticulations, and multiple calcified micronodules. In the right upper lobe, solid nodule measures 4 mm (image 55, series 5). Multiple micronodules measure up to 2 mm, mostly in the interlobar pleura. No consolidation or pleural effusion.

Mediastinum and pulmonary hila: No masses or lymphadenopathy. Small hiatal hernia. 

Heart: Enlarged heart chambers. No significant pericardial effusion. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. Aortic valve calcification. Mitral valve clipping.

Great vessels: Thoracic aorta is not dilated. Calcified atherosclerotic plaques in the wall of the thoracic aorta. Main pulmonary artery is not dilated.

Skeletal structures and chest wall: Degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. Decreased density of vertebral bodies: T11 - 82 HU, T12 - 57 HU, L1 - 67 HU, L2 - 122 HU (CT signs of demineralization).

Upper abdomen: In segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, a heterogeneous round lesion with partially calcified walls and well-defined smooth margins, measures up to 1.4 cm in diameter, density 16 HU. Common bile duct is dilated up to 1.1 cm.
val_impression:
1. In both lungs pleuroapical calcified scarring and multiple calcified micronodules may represent post-inflammatory changes/healed granulomatous disease. Solid nodule measures 4 mm, multiple micronodules. Fleischner Society recommendations may not be applicable considering the clinical history (history of skin/face cancer). Follow-up per oncology follow-up protocol.

2. Coronary artery calcification in the right coronary artery, left main coronary artery, and left anterior descending artery, qualitatively assessed as moderate. The patient is advised to consult with their primary care physician for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment or quantitative ordinal assessment of coronary artery calcification for each of the four main coronary arteries. Aortic valve calcification.

3. Decreased vertebral body density (CT signs of demineralization).

4. Hepatic cyst with peripheral focal areas of calcification in segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe, probably benign. ACR White Paper recommendations (Gore, et al. J Am Coll Radiol 2017;14:1429-1437) suggest the following: No need for further follow-up.

5. Dilated bile ducts.

DISCLAIMER: AI lung nodule detection was utilized on this study. This system is designed for high sensitivity, potentially identifying benign findings. Please refer to the radiologist's interpretation to assess the clinical significance of any detected findings.
